title: Vintage Workbench Console
summary: A decorative entryway or bathroom console styled after an old factory workbench, with splayed 45°-cut feet, open-frame legs, a lower shelf, and a chunky 2x4 tabletop trimmed in 2x6.
description:
This is a look-alike, not a working bench — the open-frame legs (built from 1x3s and 1x4s with the joints hidden on the inside via pocket holes) exist because they're easier to source and assemble than solid 4x4 posts, not because the piece needs a workbench's real structural mass. If you'd rather use solid 4x4 legs instead, the plan supports it: swap the 2x2 top-of-leg support for a 1x4, make the legs 30" long, and add a matching 1x4 to the bottom.

The splayed feet are the signature detail — both the 19-1/2" and 21-1/2" foot pieces get 45°/30° angled end cuts so the long-point-to-long-point measurement stays true to the cut list even though the ends aren't square. Sand the rounded foot ends in stages, coarse to fine, for a worn, vintage look rather than a sharp factory edge.

cutList.note: Ends cut at 45°, long point to long point stays 19-1/2".
Ends cut at 30° off square, long point to long point stays 21-1/2".

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Cut everything before you assemble anything, working from the cut list. From the 2x4s: five tabletop boards at 61", one 64" trim, one 48-1/2" end trim, one 19" end short trim, one 13" short trim, two foot top pieces at 19-1/2" with both ends at 45deg, and two foot bottom pieces at 21-1/2" with both ends 30deg off square — on both foot pairs the stated length is long point to long point, so set the saw once and cut both of a pair without moving the fence. From the 1x4s cut four leg fronts and backs at 30-3/4", two bottom-of-leg pieces at 15-1/2", and four 3-1/2" square soles; from the 1x3s cut four leg sides at 30-3/4"; from the 2x2 cut two top-of-leg supports at 13". From the 1x12 cut the 48" shelf bottom, from the 1x6 two shelf sides at 49-1/2" and two shelf ends at 11-1/2", and from the 2x6 a 14" front piece for the towel bar plus a 4" short piece. Check your stock before the first cut: each 61" tabletop board consumes a whole 8 ft 2x4, and neither the 64" nor the 48-1/2" trim will fit in one of those offcuts or share a board with the other, so the 2x4 parts need seven 8 ft boards rather than the six listed — and the two 49-1/2" shelf sides total 99", so one 8 ft 1x6 will not cover them either.

steps.title: Build the feet
steps.body: Pair one 19-1/2" top piece with one 21-1/2" bottom piece and glue and screw them face to face with 2-1/2" screws, so the shorter piece sits on top of the longer one and the splayed profile falls back from the ground on both ends. Build two of these — one per leg assembly. Keep the long points of the two pieces aligned to the same reference end as you clamp, because the angled ends are what set the splay and a 1/4" offset there shows plainly on a finished piece. Then sand the exposed end grain and the sharp corners into a soft round, starting coarse and stepping down through the grits; the worn, radiused end is the vintage detail and a crisp factory edge reads wrong.

steps.title: Attach the feet soles
steps.body: Glue and fasten a 3-1/2" square 1x4 sole flat under each end of both foot assemblies — four soles total, two per foot. The soles are what the console actually stands on, so set them flush with the outside of the foot's long point and check that both ends of a foot sit in the same plane before the glue grabs. Use 1-1/4" brad nails, or short screws, driven up through the sole into the foot so nothing shows on the floor side.

steps.title: Build the legs
steps.body: Each leg is a hollow post 30-3/4" tall, built from two 1x4 faces at 30-3/4" and two 1x3 sides at 30-3/4"; running the 2-1/2" 1x3 sides between the 3-1/2" 1x4 front and back gives a post 3-1/2" wide by 4" deep. Build four identical posts. Drill 3/4" pocket holes along the long edges of the 1x3 sides and drive 1-1/4" pocket screws from inside the post outward into the 1x4s, so every screw hole is trapped inside the hollow and none of it shows once the post is closed up. Clamp each post at both ends and at the middle while you drive, or the long thin faces will bow away from the sides and the post will finish out of square.

steps.title: Add the leg supports
steps.body: Join the four posts into two leg assemblies of two posts each. The 13" 2x2 support ties the pair together at the top and the 15-1/2" 1x4 ties them at the bottom; note that these two lengths differ by 2-1/2", so decide before you drill whether each piece fits between the posts or laps across their outside faces, and use the same choice on both assemblies. Fasten with 2" screws and glue, driving through the posts into the 2x2 and through the 1x4 into the posts. Check each assembly for square across its diagonals before the glue sets — the top support is what the tabletop later screws into, so a twisted assembly puts the top out of level.

steps.title: Attach the feet to the legs
steps.body: Stand one foot assembly under each leg assembly and fasten it with 2-1/2" screws driven down through the bottom of each post into the foot. Center the foot side to side under the pair of posts so the splay reaches out evenly in both directions. Set both finished leg assemblies on a flat floor together and confirm they stand at the same height and neither rocks before you go on; correcting a foot later means taking the whole base apart.

steps.title: Build the bottom shelf
steps.body: The shelf is a shallow open tray. The two 49-1/2" 1x6 sides run the full outside length and the 48" 1x12 bottom sits between them — 48" plus 3/4" of side stock at each end is exactly the 49-1/2" side length, which is what fixes the assembly this way round. Drill pocket holes along both long edges of the bottom, stand a side against each edge with the bottom's face flush to the sides' bottom edges, and drive 1-1/4" pocket screws. Clamp the pair as you go so the tray does not spring out of parallel down its length.

steps.title: Attach the shelf ends
steps.body: Close the two open ends of the tray with the 11-1/2" 1x6 shelf ends, pocket-screwed into both the bottom and the sides with 1-1/4" screws. Measure the actual opening at each end of the assembled tray before you fasten these — the cut list gives the ends at 11-1/2" while the bottom is 11-1/4" wide, so trim to the real opening rather than forcing the stated length in. Set the ends flush with the bottom's underside so the shelf sits flat on the leg supports later.

steps.title: Build the tabletop
steps.body: Lay the five 61" 2x4s on edge, side by side, to make a top 61" long and 17-1/2" wide (five boards at 3-1/2"). Drill pocket holes along the mating edges of four of the boards, spacing them every 8" to 10" and staggering them so screws from adjacent seams do not collide, then draw the boards together with 2-1/2" pocket screws and glue. Work on a flat surface and clamp each seam flush across the face before driving, because any step between boards has to be sanded out of a 1-1/2" thick top afterward.

steps.title: Attach the tabletop trim
steps.body: Trim the tabletop with the 2x4 pieces, gluing and screwing each with 2-1/2" screws driven through the trim into the edge of the top. The 64" piece is the one length the geometry fixes: 61" of tabletop plus 1-1/2" of trim at each end, so it caps the full front and the end trim tucks in behind it. Fit the 48-1/2", 19" and 13" pieces to the remaining edges by measuring the assembled top rather than trusting the stated lengths — the source does not say which edge each one covers. Keep every trim piece flush with the top face so the whole surface sands as one plane.

steps.title: Attach the final trim and towel bar
steps.body: Fasten the 14" 2x6 to the front of the trimmed top with 2-1/2" screws and glue, and add the 4" 2x6 short piece alongside it. This is the board the towel bar mounts to, so drive the screws where the bar's mounting plates will not land on them. Mark the bar's mounting-hole centers off the bar itself, drill pilot holes, and mount it — pine strips easily, so pilot every hole rather than driving straight into the face.

steps.title: Join the base to the top and finish
steps.body: Set the tabletop upside down on a padded surface, stand both leg assemblies on it in position, and drive 2-1/2" screws up through the 2x2 top-of-leg supports into the underside of the top. Slide the shelf tray into place between the leg assemblies and fasten it through the leg posts. Turn the console upright, sand the whole piece through the grits — a 2x4 top takes real sanding to level the seams — and apply your finish.

images.alt: A vintage-styled console table with splayed feet, open-frame legs, and a chunky 2x4 tabletop
